data.title: Surging Jobless Claims And In Line CPI Validate Our Bullish Rate Outlook
data.description:
The 10-year treasury yield dipped below 4% briefly after jobless claims came in at 263,000, which is the highest level since the end of the pandemic in late 2021. Jobless claims are a leading indicator of the labor market, whereas the actual monthly employment report is a lagging indicator, particularly given a trend of large negative revisions. CPI Core printed in line at .3, with the data series continuing to be distorted by the archaic and arbitrary calculation of shelter, with Owner’s Equivalent Rent inexplicable rising to .4% and 4% Y/Y with actual market rents approximately flat. CPI-R core, which utilizes real time data to estimate shelter vs. the two-year delayed data used in CPI-R, is running at only 1.7% (see figure below). Volatile components of CPI caused the core rate to be elevated, with airline fares rising 5.9%, used cars up 1% and motor vehicle maintenance up 2.4%.

CPI-R continues to be a leading indicator for CPI-U

We continue to be bullish on 10-year treasuries with a 3.5-4.0% year-end target based on the fact that the interest-sensitive sectors of the US economy, housing and construction, are in recession, which is impacting the job market (see figure below). This weakening of the job market will force the Fed to cut 3 times this year and reach the neutral rate of 2.75-3.0% in the first half of 2026. The US 10-year is 70% correlated with the expected terminal rate of Fed Funds, which is currently 3.00%, with a normal spread being 1.05% (see figure below). We are also bullish on stocks as Fed rate cuts and momentum in AI drive stock prices, with our upside target on the S&P at 7,000, which represents 23x our 2026 S&P earnings estimate.

Market implied policy rate continues to forecast 10-year yields

Disclosure

This information is not an offer to sell, or solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product, security, or services offered by Jay Hatfield, or Infrastructure Capital Advisors, LLC, (“ICA”) or its affiliates. ICA, will only conduct such solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product or service offered by ICA, if at all, by (1) purported definitive documentation (which will include disclosures relating to investment objective, policies, risk factors, fees, tax implications and relevant qualifications), (2) to qualified participants, if applicable, and (3) only in those jurisdictions where permitted by law. Jay Hatfield or ICA may have a beneficial long or short position in securities discussed either through stock ownership, options, or other derivatives; nonetheless, under no circumstances does any article or interview represent a recommendation to buy or sell these securities. This discussion is intended to provide insight into stocks and the market for entertainment and information purposes only and is not a solicitation of any kind. ICA buys and sells securities on behalf of its fund investors and may do so, before and after any particular article herein is published, with respect to the securities discussed in any article posted. ICA's appraisal of a company (price target) is only one factor that affects its decision whether to buy or sell shares in that company. Other factors might include, but are not limited to, the presence of mandatory limits on individual positions, decisions regarding portfolio exposures, and general market conditions and liquidity needs. As such, there may not always be consistency between the views expressed here and ICA's trading or holdings on behalf of its fund investors. There may be conflicts between the content posted or discussed and the interests of ICA. Please reach out to the ICA for more information. Investors should make their own decisions regarding any investments mentioned, and their prospects based on such investors’ own review of publicly available information and should not rely on the information contained herein. data.title: Strattec Security: Why I'm Still Bullish Despite Guidance Caution
data.description:
I reviewed Strattec Security Corporation (NASDAQ:STRT) back on July 12. The company has been a top quant-rated stock. At the time I agreed with the quant rating and said the stock was a buy. Since that time, the stock is up nearly 6%. It has had some volatility in the time period, hitting a high of $83 a share, a 30% increase in 1 month. It has since come back down to close at $67.38 at the time of writing. My buy rating at the time was warranted with the return that Strattec provided following the article.

There were a variety of reasons that I liked the stock: growing revenues, streamlined operations, improving margins and profitability, growing cash position, steady revenues, etc. The company has reported on another quarter of results since I last reviewed the stock. It is time to see if the same story holds for Strattec.

Company Overview

I reviewed the company operations in more detail in my previous article. You can refer back to that article if you want a more in-depth breakdown of the company operations. In short, Strattec is a niche automotive supplier specializing in access control, security systems, and user interface controls. Its primary customers include North American OEMs, direct sales to OEMs accounted for 90 percent of the business in 2025. The remainder of sales are from the OEM service channels, aftermarket, and sales to certain non-automotive customers.

The company manufactures in the US and Mexico, with the high majority of products coming out of Mexico. Most of the company’s products fall under the USMCA. This helps avoid tariffs on many of the products. This will be discussed later.

Continued Progress

The company financials show that the company has continued to improve the efficiency of its operations. Revenue has continued to climb, albeit slowly, but the real gains are in the margins, profitability, and cash flows. The company has been able to streamline operations.

Revenue for Q4 was $152 million, an increase of 6.3% from the prior year. For 2025, the company had revenue of $565.1 million compared to $537.8 million in the prior year, a growth rate of 5.6% for the year. Revenue continued its growth, as noted previously, the revenue growth is not the strength, but it is an important aspect. I do not want to invest in a company with declining revenues. Also, as revenues continue to grow, the company is able to leverage operations and size much more efficiently to improve margins.

Growing margins was one of the things that I liked about the company when I first reviewed it. The company has been able to continue the improvement in their gross margin. Gross margin in Q4 was 16.7%, up from 13% in the prior year and up from 16% in the prior quarter. For the year the company reported a gross margin of 15%, up from 12.2% in the prior year. The gross margin continued to improve each quarter throughout the year. Even if the company levels off at the gross margin of 16.7% seen in Q4, then it will have an improvement of 170 basis points from 2024. This is probably the most bullish aspect from the latest quarterly report. Despite an already strong increase in the gross margin last quarter, the company continued to improve upon it.

A direct correlation to improved margins is improved profitability. The company has been generating a strong profit. The year-over-year comparison is hard here since the company had a one time reduction in SAE (selling, administrative, engineering) costs of $4.7 million. This skews the numbers to be not as positive on a year over year comparison, as well as for the year total. Regardless, the company saw its operating profit increase from $20.2 million to $24.6 million. That is an increase of 21.8% year-over-year. If you back out the $4.7 million one-time recovery, then the company saw its operating profit increase by 57%.

The one concern that popped up this quarter is the large increase in SAE expenses. Year over year these expenses nearly doubled. Now, that does include the $4.7 million one time recovery that reduced the cost from Q4 last year. Despite this, the company still saw a significant increase in the amount of SAE expenses. It was not a one quarter difference, but rather an increase throughout the whole year. Even with adding back the $4.7 million to the prior year, the company saw its SAE expenses increase by 18% on the year. Much of this increase was awarded due to performance compensation as well as some costs involved with transforming the company as it operates. Now those are in effect I would like to see the company streamline this part of the business as well. The company has been able to streamline a lot of operations and improve efficiencies in a lot of areas, next year we should see this in the SAE.

Streamlined operations, along with the other factors discussed, have driven cash flows for the company. The company increased cash provided by operating activities from $12.3 million to $71.7 million. Yes, that is an increase of 483%. Cash from operations was $30.2 million in Q4. There is no one-off anomaly in the data driving that number either. The company notes the following in its 10-Q, "the increase in cash provided by operating activities was due to reduced purchasing levels on higher sales, collection of accounts receivable, and the recovery of pre-production costs".

We can see some of this increase coming from the Primary Working Capital Management. The company uses primary working capital as a percentage of sales (PWC %) as a key metric of working capital management. The company defines this metric as the sum of net accounts receivable and net inventory less accounts payable, divided by the past three months sales annualized. The table below outlines the PWC%:

The company was able to free up $25 million in operating cash flow by streamlining its working capital. That is a lot of extra cash for the company. That reduction might be hard to replicate again in 2026, but I do expect the company to continue to improve on that front and keep more cash freed up.

Cash and Debt

Another point of strength that has come about due to the continued increase in profit is the cash position. The company ended their FY2025 with $84.6 million in cash. The company only has $8 million in debt that sits under a revolving credit line. That leaves it with a net cash position of $76.6 million.

The company expects to spend approximately $13 million in 2026 on capital expenditures. That amount can easily be covered from cash flows. The company had free cash flow of $64.5 million in 2025 with capex of $7.2 million. This means the company, barring a large setback, should continue to build its cash position in 2026.

The company did not discuss any plans for a share buyback or a dividend, at least nothing definitive in the near future. The company noted the following in the conference call: “Over the longer term, once we've established a greater amount of predictability in the business, we'll be in a better position to consider shareholder distributions as well as M&A.” I think this primarily comes due to the concerns and volatility in the auto market. It is expected that the auto market will see a slowdown in the coming year. The company wants to keep its balance sheet in a position of strength in case there is a slowdown that has a significant effect on the financials. The other reason is that the company has not historically been as profitable as it was in 2025. The company has been able to streamline operations and improve profitability to the point where it is generating strong cash flow. I would expect the company to be cautious with its cash due to market forecasts. I also expect the company to continue to generate positive cash flow and would not be surprised to see a form of return to shareholders.

Outlook and Guidance

The company performed great in 2025 as a whole and also exceptionally well in Q4 of 2025. Strattec grew revenues all while it streamlined operations, improved margins, and managed cash better. When investing you are always looking at what is coming rather than just behind. The company saw its stock price peak at $83 a share just before earnings. Following earnings the stock price fell 19% to reach the current price of $67.3. Much of this decline came about due to the guidance and outlook provided by Strattec.

A lot of not that great coming out of the guidance. The company was able to achieve a lot in 2025 transforming the business into a profitable and cash generating company. Much of those achievements will make the comps in 2026 much tougher. The low fruit has been picked and things are expected to level off.

The first big one is that revenue is expected to be flat or a modest decline. The company also noted this during their conference call and prepared remarks.

“Looking forward, we are excited about our longer-term future but recognize in the near term we face market uncertainty. North American automotive production estimates have recently been revised downward, coupled with a lull in the launch cycle of new vehicles by our key customers.”

This is partly why the company is holding onto its cash position for the time being. The company is prepared for a slowdown in their growth. They want to make sure they have the cash available to invest in the business even if things do slow down.

I still think the company will have a strong year as they expect to continue to improve margins, even if it is more minor for the year. That improvement will pass through to the bottom line for the year. Management noted that they think the business can consistently run at 18-20% gross margin. Ending the year at 16.7% it leaves plenty of room for improvement. Even if revenues are flat for the year if the gross margin improves by 50 basis points that is almost an additional $3 million in profit.

Estimates have Strattec projected to earn $4.25 a share in 2026 a decline from the $4.58 in 2025. I have a hard time seeing the EPS falling by that much for the year. If the company is able to generate flat revenue it will still have better margins meaning higher EPS. I did not see anything from the company that would lead me to think there should be a decent sized decline in EPS. Management made it clear that they were cautious about 2026 and the potential market slowdown. That being said they provided guidance for a flat to slight decline in revenues with expected improvements in margins. Also the other factor not yet discussed is tariffs. The company noted that tariff costs were $5-$7 million before mitigation efforts. They also noted that they expect to be able to recover most of the tariff costs but that the reimbursement will lag the expenses. This means there could be improvement next year with the recovery of some of the tariff costs. This would boost profits as well.

Risks

The biggest risk for Strattec continues to revolve around the macro for the automotive market. It is expected that the market will see a slowdown in 2026. The company projects this will cause flat to minor declines in the revenue. Tariffs are a wildcard that will continue to weigh on the industry as well. The company has taken measures to mitigate the direct impacts, but there will continue to be indirect impacts, such as decreased car sales.

The company continues to see high concentration among its customers. It was noted by management that they continue to expand their customer base and hope to improve on that front. Doing so would reduce the risk of one company seeing a slump in sales affecting Strattec.

Conclusion

I liked Strattec when I reviewed the company a couple of months ago. It is a company that is growing revenues, improving margins, and growing its cash flows. It has a stronghold for a balance sheet that provides it room to invest and weather any slowdowns. This should also allow it to return profits to shareholders in the future as well.

The company saw a strong run-up in price and then a decline following earnings. This is mostly due to estimates dampening the growth story. The automotive market is expected to go through a slowdown; this equates to guidance of flat to minor decline in revenue growth. Regardless of this the company is expected to pull through its gains on the margin side of the business. I think this will allow the company to beat earnings estimates for 2026.

I do not think the story has changed for Strattec. I continue to rate Strattec a buy.

data.title: Tsakos Energy Navigation: Strategic Fleet Management And Robust Liquidity For Better Positioning
data.description:
Three months after my last article on Tsakos Energy Navigation Limited (NYSE:TEN), external factors remain unfavorable amid weaker oil prices and stubborn inflation. Despite this, TEN exudes resilience with its prudent fleet management to remain efficient and profitable. Its Balance Sheet remains robust, promising sustainability and dividend longevity. These aspects justify its current valuation even after the 10% increase from my last coverage. Technicals are still bullish, although a dip is possible after the Q2 2025 results.

TEN Q2 2025: Challenges Are Still Evident

Tsakos Energy Navigation Limited faced weaker yet more volatile external factors, which led to its softer performance on a YoY basis. Its Q2 2025 revenue amounted to $193.3M, down by -9.8% YoY from $214.1M. Its revenue also weakened by -2.0% from my previous coverage at $197.1M.

This should not be surprising considering the continued decline in oil prices. During Q2 2025, WTI and Brent hit their one-year low at $57-58 per barrel and $60-61 per barrel, respectively. This was worsened by the OPEC oil hike, which reduced the impact of its sudden price uptick in June. Prices rose and exceeded $70 when the Israel-Iran tension broke out and led to the Strait of Hormuz Blockade. However, this was very short-lived, as ceasefire news easily pushed the price to sub-$70 again. Considering this, its shipping rates or revenues per day were negatively affected. In fact, its time charter equivalent, or TCE Rate decreased from $34,235 to $30,767 YoY.

On a lighter note, its utilization rate rose noticeably amid its timely fleet deliveries and lower dry-dockings. Unexpected reroutes and disruptions amid the Israel-Iran tension may have also opened opportunities for TEN to capture more demand. After all, it has a significant global presence with 62 vessels operating during the quarter. This partially offset the impact of weaker TCE rates. The decrease in TCE could have also driven it since it means that TEN’s services became more affordable.

Moreover, TEN worked on its cost management despite the impact of the geopolitical tension on shipping crew salaries and insurance, broker commissions, and canal tolls. It is also worth noting that cost pressures remained intense as inflation picked up its momentum to 2.7%. Excluding the proceeds from the disposal or sale of vessels, its operating expenses remained flat. While this was not enough to completely offset the decrease in revenue, TEN maintained a double-digit operating margin of 25.9%. This was enough to cover the remaining expenses and taxes.

Oil Prices Will Remain A Double-Edged Sword

Oil price volatility will still be a primary challenge for TEN’s shipping rates. Now, WTI moves within $62-65, while Brent is at $65-68. Next month, OPEC will reduce its output hike to 137,000 barrels per day. This can help push oil prices up. However, the actual impact may not be that much. After all, OPEC made a larger-than-expected oil production hike last month. Inflation and tariff woes still signal uncertainty, affecting oil pricing and demand today. Despite this, TEN may still benefit from it through lower fuel expenses. This can partially offset the impact on revenues.

The Israel-Qatar Tension

After Iran, Israel now faces tension with Qatar after attacking the latter’s capital city. We know that Qatar has played an important role in mediation efforts and indirect ceasefire talks in Gaza. While this tension does not automatically disrupt activities in strategic maritime zones like the Strait of Hormuz and Red Sea, this can still affect TEN and other oil tankers.

One is the potential increase in war insurance costs and crew wages. This may also lead to targeted risks in key routes. The possibility of reroutes should not be discounted.

On a lighter note, it can push oil prices up and boost its average daily rates. After all, its time charter employment rate is not completely fixed. Also, it still has some spot market exposure. Reroutes may also open opportunities for TEN to capture temporary demand as consumers look for alternative tankers.

TEN Is Well-Positioned

Despite these double-edged swords, TEN remains well-positioned today with its prudent fleet management. Out of 24 tankers, three have already been delivered. Another one will be delivered in Q4 2025. Another three will be delivered in 2026. Six will be delivered in 2027. While the remaining 11 are expected to arrive by 2028. With that, TEN’s current operating capacity and delivery timeline makes sense considering the potential global economic and oil market recovery in these years. Aside from that, it still has plenty of vessels with a decent average age to manage potential repairs and maintenance expenses. Also, having newer vessels can improve fuel efficiency.

Another factor is its decreasing exposure to spot rates. As we can see, TEN is becoming more exposed to TCE while reducing spot rate days. This allows it to improve freight rate and revenue stability. This is very crucial at this time amid volatile oil prices, macroeconomic uncertainty, and geopolitical tensions.

Moreover, I expect more opportunities for LNG carriers in the long run. With the global data center expansion and increasing capacity, electricity consumption may spike in the following years. I expect natural gas to maintain high global demand despite increasing calls for renewables. After all, it is more scalable than renewables, much safer than nuclear energy, and easier to produce than coal. LNG will be needed by countries with no access to natural gas, so TEN’s LNG carriers will be crucial in the long run.

With all these growth prospects and expansion opportunities, it is important to note that TEN maintains enough capital buffer to sustain its capacity. Its cash level remains adequate and relatively stable at $287.2M. Although debt is still high, the maturity profile remains long. Its Net Debt/EBITDA of 4.4x is still decent and lower than the standard maximum of 4.5x for capital-intensive companies. This means that the company can pay all borrowings at once even if it doesn’t have to. I believe TEN can cover its repayments since its operations continue to generate positive profits.

Valuation

Considering the recent changes in its performance, I decided to update the DCF Model. I increased its perpetual growth rate to 2.0% to be consistent with inflation. WACC also increased after adjusting the risk premium from just 4.5% to 5.5% to cover more risks and be more conservative. Considering this, the TP decreased from my previous TP of $25.05 to $24.48. Despite this, the TP still suggests an upside potential of at least 10%, proving that TEN is still reasonably valued today.

Technicals

TEN remains bullish despite the price decrease right after the Q2 2025 release. As we can see, the price continues to increase and set new highs and lows above the 50-day and 200-day SMA. Also, the 50-day SMA is still trending upward, which means that market sentiments are still optimistic.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI remain consistent with the price trend, so a bearish divergence is not detected yet. However, its potential dip is evident as the MACD line appears to be crossing below the signal line. The histogram is still within positive territory but decreasing close to zero or neutral. This means two things. One is that buying is decreasing. Another is that selling volume is outpacing buying volume. This may be due to profit-takers after the stock became nearly overbought prior to the Q2 2025 release. Despite this, the dip shown by its RSI shows new buying opportunities as it drops far below the overbought zone.

Conclusion

Tsakos Energy Navigation Limited remains well-positioned with its prudent fleet management and decreasing spot rate exposure. This is supported by its robust Balance Sheet, which promises sustainability and dividend longevity. In addition, the stock is still cheap with some upside potential. Technicals show a probability of a dip but remain bullish. TEN is still a solid stock, but I’m downgrading it from strong buy to buy as it gets closer to my DCF TP and market volatility persists.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: Klarna Leads Busiest Week For IPOs Since 2021, As More Names Join The Pipeline
data.description:
Seven sizable IPOs debuted this week, the most in nearly four years, joined by 3 SPACs. 10 IPOs and 6 SPACS submitting initial filings.

Buy-now-pay-later e-commerce platform Klarna (KLAR) priced above its range to raise $1.4 billion at a market cap of $15.3 billion, in the fourth largest US IPO of 2025 so far. Klarna is best known for its buy-now-pay-later plans, including its four-part interest-free payment plan. It also offers its own "Klarna Card" in partnership with Visa, and its own app offering cashback on e-commerce purchases made through it. Klarna finished the week up 7%.

Blockchain-powered lending platform Figure Technology (FIGR) priced above its upwardly revised range to raise $788 million at a market cap of $6.1 billion. The company offers a vertically integrated suite of blockchain-based solutions that powers its marketplaces across lending, trading, and investing, originating loans directly to borrowers while also enabling mortgage originators, servicers, banks, brokers, and credit unions to originate and distribute loans through its partner-branded technology platform. Figure finished the week up 30%.

Neuropsychiatry biotech LB Pharmaceuticals (LBRX) priced its upsized IPO at the midpoint to raise $285 million at a market cap of $338 million. The phase-3 ready biotech’s lead candidate is LB-102, an oral small molecule that it is developing to treat acute schizophrenia. After achieving its primary endpoint at week four in its 359-patient Phase 2 trial, LB Pharmaceuticals expects to begin a Phase 3 trial in the 1Q26. The listing was the first major biotech IPO since February. LB Pharmaceuticals finished the week up 12%.

Drive-thru coffee chain Black Rock Coffee Bar (BRCB) priced above its range to raise $294 million at a market cap of $956 million. The company is an operator of drive-thru and sit-in coffee bars offering various types of caffeinated beverages. It has 158 locations spanning seven states as of June 30, 2025. Black Rock Coffee Bar ended the week up 38%.

Crypto exchange Gemini (GEMI) priced above its upwardly revised range to raise $425 million at a market cap of $3.5 billion. The company is a regulated cryptocurrency exchange serving retail and institutional clients in over 60 countries, offering spot and derivatives trading, staking, OTC trading, custody, a stablecoin, and other crypto-related products. As of June 30, 2025, Gemini had 523,000 monthly transacting users, 10,000 institutions, $18 billion in crypto under custody, and $285 billion in lifetime trading volume. Gemini ended the week up 14%.

Systems services provider Legence (LGN) priced above the midpoint to raise $728 million at a market cap of $2.9 billion. The company provides engineering, installation, and maintenance services for mission-critical systems in buildings, specializing in designing, fabricating, and installing complex HVAC, process piping, and other mechanical, electrical, and plumbing systems for new facilities, and upgrading HVAC, lighting, and building controls in existing facilities. Legence ended the week up 9%.

Public transit services platform Via Transportation (VIA) priced above its range to raise $493 million at a market cap of $4.2 billion. The company develops technology for public transit agencies and operators to plan, manage, and optimize services such as microtransit, school transport, and paratransit. Its platform combines scheduling, dispatch, compliance, passenger booking, and data analytics, with optional services like fleet procurement and call center support. Via ended the week up 8%.

Three SPACs listed this week. Talon Capital (TLNCU), led by the founder of CSL Capital Management, raised $225 million to target the energy sector. Trailblazer Acquisition (BLZRU) raised $240 million to target media, entertainment, tech, and retail. XIP-backed OTG Acquisition I (OTGAU) raised $200 million to target digital infrastructure.

While not included in the table below, Chinese fintech products provider Zenta Group (ZGM) priced its IPO at the bottom of the range to raise $6 million at a $46 million market cap. It ended the week up 2%.

Ten IPOs, mostly small issuers, submitted initial filings this week. Leading the pack, commercial washing machine manufacturer Alliance Laundry Holdings (ALH) filed for an IPO that could raise an estimated $500 million. Two other sizable filings came from auto loan platform Lendbuzz (LBZZ), which filed for an IPO that could raise an estimated $250 million, and power and data center infrastructure REIT Fermi (FRMI), which filed to raise $150 million.

Six SPACS also submitted initial filings this week. Bain Capital GSS Investment (BCSS.U), backed by Bain Capital, filed to raise $400 million. Meshflow Acquisition (MFLWU.RC) filed to raise $300 million to target the blockchain and digital assets. QDRO Acquisition (QADRU) filed to raise $200 million to target financial services. Daedalus Special Acquisition (DSACU) filed to raise $200 million to target AI and technology. Range Capital Acquisition II (RNGTU) filed to raise $200 million to target capital-constrained sectors. Shreya Acquisition (SHRYU.RC) filed to raise $60 million, targeting the hospitality, wellness, and media sectors.

Week Ahead

Four sizable IPOs are currently scheduled to price in the week ahead, led by well-known ticket marketplace StubHub (STUB).

StubHub plans to raise $800 million at a $9.5 billion market cap. The company operates a global secondary ticketing marketplace for live events, using the brand StubHub in North America and viagogo internationally. StubHub has strong brand awareness, and expansion into original ticket sales is a large opportunity. However, it has faced legal and regulatory challenges, particularly related to pricing.

Cybersecurity firm Netskope (NTSK) plans to raise $765 million at a $7.5 billion market cap. Netskope One is a unified, cloud-native platform supported by the company's NewEdge global private cloud network, comprised of 120+ full-compute edge data centers across 75+ regions. The company has delivered strong growth and has a sticky customer base, but it remains highly unprofitable with a large accumulated deficit.

Oilfield services provider WaterBridge (WBI) plans to raise $500 million at a $2.1 billion market cap. The company operates the largest produced water infrastructure network in the US, serving customers such as Permian Resources and Chevron. Most of its revenue comes from long-term contracts, providing good visibility, though its business is capital-intensive and indirectly exposed to commodity price fluctuations.

Pattern Group (PTRN) plans to raise $300 million at a $2.6 billion market cap. Pattern calls itself "the pioneer of ecommerce acceleration" with its own proprietary technology platform powered by AI and machine learning. The company is pitching a combination of accelerating growth and profitability, with expanding margins and cash flow. Its sales model does subject it to consumer and inflation risks, though, and it faces significant competition for both tech and products.

The Renaissance IPO Indices are market cap-weighted baskets of newly public companies. As of 9/11/2025, the Renaissance IPO Index was up 22.1% year-to-date, while the S&P 500 was up 13.0%. Renaissance Capital's IPO ETF (IPO) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Astera Labs (ALAB) and Reddit (RDDT). The Renaissance International IPO Index was up 38.3% year-to-date, while the ACWX was up 25.0%. Renaissance Capital’s International IPO ETF (IPOS) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Galderma and Horizon Robotics.

data.title: PSF: Potential Catalyst Ahead But Still Uncertain Of Dividend Coverage
data.description:
Overview

Income focused funds can be an efficient way for investors to hedge their portfolio against the market volatility and uncertainty that traditional equities can expose investors to. This is why Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund (NYSE:PSF) can be a great option for investors that want to prioritize capital preservation and focus on consistent income through all market cycles. When I previously covered PSF, I issued a hold rating due to the weak dividend coverage. However, PSF has released an updated semi-annual report for 2025 and this prompted me to revisit the fund to provide some updated insights about its performance and outlook for the remainder of 2025.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that PSF's share price has increased by about 0.6%. The fund has been able to ride the momentum of the market rally over recent months to recover from its prior decline. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return sits around 8.26% over the same time frame. One of the main appeals of PSF is the starting dividend yield of about 7.4% and monthly payouts, which makes it an enticing choice for income investors. According to the latest semi-annual report, PSF is on track to support distributions for remainder of 2025.

Due to the structure of PSF's portfolio, the valuation continues to remain suppressed during this period of elevated interest rates. As a result, PSF still trades at a favorable discount to NAV valuation. Now that interest rate cuts are anticipated to take place this month, PSF may have a short-term catalyst to help lift the share price before the end of the year. Although we don't know the size of these estimated interest rate cuts yet, a lower cost of capital could improve the market sentiment around income focused investments. So let's start by taking a look at the fund strategy that PSF implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the most recent fact sheet, PSF has total managed assets of $388.3M that is spread across 306 different holdings. The fund allocates the majority of its assets to preferred investments and income securities that are issued by US and non-US companies. Therefore, PSF can be an efficient way for income investors to diversify their portfolio away from dependence on the success of US markets. The full breakdown of the portfolio by region is as follows:

  • United States: 47%
  • Canada: 12%
  • Other: 11%
  • United Kingdom: 10%
  • France: 8%
  • Switzerland: 4%
  • Spain: 3%
  • Netherlands: 3%

Expanding on this diversity, PSF allocates its assets across a few different sectors. However, the fund is most heavily allocated to securities in the Banking sector at 54% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to Insurance and Utilities investments, which account for 13% and 12% of assets, respectively. This primary allocation to the Banking sector could be both a source of vulnerability and a benefit, depending on market conditions. So investors should be aware of the fund's concentration risk to this one sector.

Since the fund is most heavily allocated to the Banking sector, the top holdings are no surprise. We can see that PSF's largest individual position is in Citigroup (C) with a weight of 1.2%. We can also see many other notable high-quality names, such as Goldman Sachs (GS), UBS Group (UBS), Barclays (BCS), and Bank of America (BAC), just to name a few. However, we must remember that PSF maintains exposure to these positions through preferred shares and other income structures, so investors should limit their expectation of PSF's ability to provide a meaningful level of capital appreciation from the growth of these assets.

To demonstrate this concept, I pulled in a performance chart of PSF over the last ten-year period. As we can see below, PSF's share price has declined by about 13.1% over the last decade, but most of the decline only happened following 2022. Prior to the interest rate hikes of 2022, PSF's share price has generally remained within a consistent range. When including all distributions, the total return jumps up to nearly 95.5% over the same period. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of the return will come from the distributions received.

Updated Valuation & Risk Profile

At the time of my last coverage, PSF traded at a discount to NAV of approximately 7.5%. Following the recent market rally, PSF now trades at a slightly smaller discount to NAV of approximately 6.75%. However, the current discount is still more attractive than the average discount to NAV of 2.6% over the last five-year period. To better conceptualize the current discount level in relation to its historical range, I added the red line in the graph below. As we can see, PSF currently trades at one of the deepest discount to NAV valuations over the last ten years, which indicates that it may be a good time to accumulate shares.

Since the time of my last coverage, PSF has released an updated semi-annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.63 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.16 per share. Assuming similar performance over the next six month period, PSF is aligned to have a strong year for net investment income. Looking back on prior years, the fund has demonstrated that it is capable of generating consistent income over time, despite shifting macroeconomic conditions.

However, the net investment income levels aren't sufficient enough to support the distributions on their own. Therefore, PSF has remained reliant on positive net realized gains to fuel NAV growth and support the payouts it makes. However, the only problem is that net realized gains are reliant on positive market conditions so that it can sell securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased for. This makes it difficult for PSF to capture positive net realized gains during market declines and leaves the fund vulnerable to market downturns. This reliance has made it difficult for the fund to provide a meaningful level of NAV growth over the last few years. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $26.81 per share
  • 2021: $25.99 per share
  • 2022: $20.48 per share
  • 2023: $20.35 per share
  • 2024: $21.53 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $21.56 per share

As part of the fund's operating strategy, PSF utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify earnings when possible. The use of leverage currently sits around 32.92% of assets, which I consider to be an aggressive use of leverage during this elevated interest rate environment. I consider this aggressive because investors must consider the risk that leverage can actually amplify losses if allocated to securities that do not perform as anticipated. 84% of the fund's leverage is structured on a fixed rate basis, with a weighted average rate of 1.5%. However, the remaining 16% of the leverage is structured on a variable rate, at an interest rate of 5.2%, which may be contributing to the fund's overall sensitivity to interest rates.

Another source of risk would be the exposure to securities with poor credit ratings. For reference, securities with a credit rating below a BBB are classified as below investment grade. These are generally borrowers with less consistent cash flows or that may be more reliant on debt to fuel operational growth. These below investment grade borrowers are more likely to expose PSF to the negative impact of defaults.

Even if PSF doesn't actually experience defaults within its own portfolio, the share price can be moved by the overall sentiment of the debt markets. For instance, S&P Global estimates that the rate of leverage loan defaults can increase as high as 3.5% by March of 2026, assuming continued trade disruptions related to tariffs. The breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 17%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 68%
  • Not Rated: 12%

Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.126 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.4%. Looking at the dividend history below, we can see that payouts have consistently declined over the last decade. This makes it difficult to confidently recommend a long-term position in PSF for income investors. The whole point of maintaining exposure to a high-yielding asset like PSF, is for the income that it can provide. If that income is consistently being decreased over time, it defeats the purpose of maintaining a position.

Referring to the same semi-annual report, it seems like PSF is on track to cover the distribution at this time. If we take the monthly distribution amount of $0.126 per share and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $1.512 per share. The earnings over the last few years have been inconsistent, but it seems like PSF doesn't produce a sufficient level of earnings as frequently as it needs. For reference, here are the year-end earnings amounts:

  • 2020: $1.45 per share
  • 2021: $1.61 per share
  • 2022:($3.89) per share
  • 2023: $1.44 per share
  • 2024: $2.69 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $0.79 per share

As we can see, PSF only generated a sufficient level of earnings in two of the last five years. Additionally, it's worth mentioning that since the distributions are fueled by net investment income, the distributions are primarily classified as ordinary dividend income. Ordinary dividends have the least favorable tax treatment for investors. So based on your specific tax bracket, the amount of capital invested, or desired income level, a position in PSF is best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to offset these potential tax consequences.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my hold rating on PSF at this time. While the fund could benefit from short-term interest rate cuts, the underlying performance is reliant on positive net realized gains and distribution coverage could be improved. Since the fund is mainly allocated to Bank investments, this could also present some concentration risk to one specific focus area. PSF trades at an attractive discount to NAV valuation, but I would prefer to stay on the sidelines to observe performance once interest rates are cut.

data.title: O-I Glass: Big Winner From Rate Cuts Ready For A Turnaround
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

O-I Glass (NYSE:OI) is a pioneer (founded by the inventor of automatic bottle-making machine) and industry leader when it comes to glass bottles and jars for a wide range of industries including food and beverages (e.g. beer, wine, spirits, and non-alcoholic beverages), as well as cosmetics and pharma. As a result, they depend significantly on the economy, but right now they seem to be recovering from the global packaging recession seen a couple of years ago, with an ambitious cost saving plan and improving performance, being in a great position for a recovery thanks also to industry tailwinds.

The company managed to navigate the relatively weak market conditions relatively well, reporting a 20% increase in EPS in Q2 2025 vs Q2 2024 and reporting a ~1% increase in volume in H1 2025.

What’s more important, however, is their Fit To Win program, which is advancing very well, prompting the increase of their 2025 guidance, now expecting adjusted EPS of $1.3 to $1.55 this year, meaning a 60% to 90% better number than last year. At the same time, they also halted the MAGMA (Modular Advanced Glass Manufacturing Asset; which was promising innovation, sustainability, modular production, and flexibility plus proximity to customers) program, as F2W is expected to provide lower capital intensity - a sign that they’re now in a self-preservation mode where they focus on costs and margins rather than trying to expand.

But, what’s more important to me is the cash flow. They expect $150 million to $200 million in FCF in 2025, significantly better than the negative $128 million recorded last year. On their $1.95 billion market cap, that’s potentially a ratio of even less than 10. Still, that’s $150 million to $200 million despite $140 to $150 million in cash restructuring costs, so the company’s real potential is significantly better.

As mentioned, their F2W strategy is very important, targeting $250 million in annual cost savings in 2025 ($145 million already achieved in H1’25) and even more than $650 million by 2027, which is a massive amount of money for a ~$1.95 billion market cap. This is planned to be achieved through a mix of reshaping SG&A expenses, network optimizations, improving organizational effectiveness and cost transformation, as can be seen above with comments and details. If they manage to achieve even $500 million of annual cost savings, this could add significant amounts of money to their cash flows and improve the margins to a point where the market would have to pay attention to it, so the potential remains significant - but the execution risk remains.

The issue here remains the company’s balance sheet. Based on their latest report, we can see a very significant amount of debt on a roughly $1.95 billion market cap, with over $5 billion in debt, although the current assets cover the current liabilities and they are improving, so it’s not really a terrible balance sheet. But again, if they manage to save so much money combined with industry tailwinds that I’ll mention below which should be directly beneficial for their margins and cash flow, they could enter into a significant debt repayment cycle, deleveraging and potentially improving the company significantly.

Dividends & Buybacks

As the company is now in a self-preservation mode marked by significant cost savings, it wouldn’t make sense to see them pump a lot of money into dividends and buybacks at first. However, I believe the optimal thing for them to do would be a mix of debt repayments combined with buybacks at a relatively low valuation, helping boost the price in the wake of a potential market recovery, which can later be used for acquisitions instead of debt. Nothing is announced in this regard though, but it’s a high-potential possibility to keep in mind, which I believe could boost the price if announced.

Risks & Opportunities

Similar to the industry it serves, O-I Glass has seen a slowdown in recent years. This is because beverages are generally a very discretionary industry, which makes it very susceptible to the economy. Right now, the whole industry has been facing issues due to Trump’s tariffs and especially higher rates seen in recent years. However, as trade agreements are reached (I don’t think tariffs are an end goal, but rather a tool for leverage in negotiations), that uncertainty will keep getting reduced.

Similar to other industries tied to the consumer, their dependency on the economy and discretionary budgets also means that rate cuts (which are now a very likely catalyst and should continue even after September) will likely be the company’s number one opportunity in the near-term. By lowering interest rates, the Fed basically increases discretionary budgets, which could be great for OI’s customers (and their clients as well) and the company itself. Not only that, but by lowering the interest payments and the cost of debt, OI could benefit significantly since they have such a high level of debt (and a record $337 million interest expense TTM), potentially unlocking significantly better cash flows even if the company itself wouldn’t grow its revenue. This can then open the potential for a nice compounding if they reinvest that money in a growing environment. Since Powell’s Jackson Hole remarks and the recent job data and much higher rate cut expectations, they seem like a nearly guaranteed catalyst at this point, which will have a clear effect on the company and its stock.

Again, to be clear, this is a well-known effect caused by rate hikes and cuts. They directly lower or increase discretionary budgets by increasing or decreasing the cost of debt (such as mortgages, credit card debt, etc. for consumers), which then ripples through the whole economy, with hikes causing lower activity which then affects companies - all in an effort to fight inflation. This is why it shouldn’t be a surprise when Constellation Brands (STZ), Moët Hennessy - Louis Vuitton (OTCPK:LVMHF), Nike (NKE), PepsiCo (PEP) and virtually every company that depends on consumers reports issues in such an environment, with Nike reporting the biggest sales drop in five years back in March 2025.

Another important aspect mentioned in the Constellation Brands analysis is that in the current environment, especially since the pandemic, we see this de-premiumization in pretty much everything that’s consumer-related, almost everywhere on the planet, from apparel, to pet food, beauty products and human food, with many companies citing weak consumer demand that led to a drop in revenue. On top of that, we saw the issues in China that virtually everyone who operates in the market reported over the past couple of years - especially in premium - but all these issues are probably not permanent, and I believe we are just witnessing normal consumer behaviour given the point of the cycle we are in, which should be moved forward thanks to rate cuts.

Especially since glass is often associated with more premium or higher end products, it’s no surprise that the company was affected by it, and I think cost savings and a self-preservation mode that we see them in right now were a good decision, preparing them to withstand this uncertainty for long. Still, beverage producers (especially alcoholic ones) have seen the trend of premiumisation for ages, and just because it slowed down now it doesn’t mean it won’t happen. This remains a significant industry tailwind for them, as consumers (especially younger) tend to prefer quality over quantity nowadays, for which the packaging plays a very important role.

Still, I’ll mention once again that until these issues are solved, we remain in an uncertain environment with high tension, very volatile market sentiment, potential escalations of a trade war and still relatively high interest rates even if they’re cut now. These will be the main things impacting the economy in the near future, so the market will pay very close attention to them, especially since mismanaging these can lead to a recession, which would be terrible for OI. As they said, back in 2023 to most of 2024, they even highlighted a “global packaging recession” due to the massive drop in demand and shipments, which is now starting to improve and should be boosted by rate cuts. In that regard, they gave some clarity during the Q2 earnings Q&A:

Overall, what we're seeing over the course of the year is a generally stable environment with maybe the exceptions of some disruption due to the capital project that Gordon talked about as well as maybe some fluctuation we saw between the first and second quarter associated with tariff concerns or uncertainties and trying to buy ahead. Other than that, if you take out the noise of kind of prior year comps and things like that, you're looking at a pretty stable environment.

So, improvements in terms of stability are already seen if we take out the noise/tariff concerns, and with rate cuts and a potential improvement in consumer demand, there could be significant turnaround potential. Plus, tariffs could actually be helpful for the company, as their local production could be a way for beverage/other producers to bypass them, potentially offsetting the risks from China and others.

Peer Comparison

For this comparison, I’ll include more than just glass packaging manufacturers to see O-I Glass from a broader, industry-wide perspective. When compared to Ardagh Metal Packaging (AMBP), Verallia Société Anonyme (OTCPK:VRLAF), Silgan Holdings (SLGN), Crown Holdings (CCK), Amcor (AMCR) and Greif (GEF), we actually see the lowest P/CF ratio (not taking into account the potential improvements coming in the following years), while their PB and most other ratios are also the lowest or nearly the lowest in the sector. Perhaps their exposure to glass packaging, which might be affected by the de-premiumisation mentioned earlier, also plays a role in this, although I don’t think it should be the case in the long-term.

Valuation

For the valuation, I’ll begin with the midpoint of $175 million FCF expected in 2025, $250 million in 2026, $350 million in 2027 and $400 million in 2028, followed by a 3% CAGR until year 10 and 2% terminal growth rate, a relatively conservative assumption that takes into account their near-term growth potential thanks to rate cuts (improving demand + lower interest expenses), lower restructuring costs and cost savings being accretive to the free cash flow. This way, we’re assuming the management doesn’t deliver on their targets for various reasons (tariffs, bad environment, etc.), while the company’s fundamental cost savings improve the margin, so the margin of safety should be quite good. Using a WACC of 9% (higher than my estimated WACC of 7.52% that assumes ~7.2% cost of equity, ~7.65% cost of debt, 35% tax rate based on their estimated 2025 level of 33% to 36%) for an even better margin of safety, along with $4.65 billion in net debt and 153.99 million shares outstanding, discounting the cash flows and terminal value gives an enterprise value of $7.90 billion, which, after subtracting net debt, implies an equity value of $3.25 billion, or $21.11 per share, which is almost identical to their peak in 2023, before the industry’s issues became apparent.

Conclusions

Overall, I rate O-I Glass is a Strong Buy now thanks to their potential turnaround, being a significant winner in case of rate cuts as a result of their balance sheet, with significant cost savings, solid cash flows and potential shareholder returns in the future. The risk remains in case the environment issues persist or management fails to deliver on their promises significantly, but their fair value seems to be fair from the current level even if we assume that they’re wrong in their expectations.

data.title: Inflation Is Not A Virus, And It's Not Going Up
data.description:
Inflation is not like a virus that spreads through a population. A rising price in one part of the economy cannot "infect" a price in another part of the economy. A currency doesn't just "catch" inflation because oil prices go up or a drought causes wheat prices to rise. Inflation is the result of an imbalance between the supply of money and the demand to hold it. It's a monetary phenomenon, as Milton Friedman taught us. And it generally results in a rising price for most goods and services. The reason inflation appeared to rise last month, as the August CPI report implied, is not because of a monetary imbalance. As the charts below will show, the bulk of the apparent rise in inflation can be traced back to the way the BLS calculates inflation in the housing sector. There's another factor at work as well: the BLS can and does make mistakes, and numbers are typically volatile from month to month. Seasonal adjustment factors can be off and in need of revision. Consumer preferences for many goods change as prices change, but the BLS is slow to pick that up. The change in one month's number does not reliably mark a change in trend.

Chart #1

The Producer Price Index for Finished Goods (Chart #1) is a great illustration of the rather explosive inflation episode which the economy experienced from early 2021 to mid-2022. Prices were rising slowly from 2015 through 2020, then suddenly rose by more about 27% from early 2021 to mid-2022, only to once again resume a slow rise from mid-2022 through today. The fuel for that price explosion was a $6 trillion increase in the M2 money supply, a subject I've covered extensively in prior posts. From mid-2022 to August 2025, prices have increased at an annualized rate of only 1.4%, with little or no sign of any meaningful acceleration. The return to low and relatively stable inflation was preceded by a dramatic contraction in M2.

Chart #2

The Final Demand version of the PPI (Chart #2) shows the same pattern. Although it rose at an annualized rate of 2.2% over the past 3 years, it is up at only a 1% annualized rate in the past six months.

Chart #3

Oil prices (Chart #3) show a rather sharp increase from 2021 through mid-2022, but they haven't increased at all since pre-Covid times. Since mid-2022, oil prices have actually fallen by 40%! Non-energy commodity prices have fallen by almost 10% since March '22. If inflation is on the rise, someone forgot to tell the commodity markets.

Chart #4

Chart #4 shows that the BLS's calculation for housing inflation today (which they call Owner's Equivalent Rent, or OER) is closely related to the year-over-year change in national home prices from 18 months prior. Today, home prices nationwide are barely increasing and are very likely to decline. Yet, the BLS calculates that housing-related costs rose 4% in the past year.

Chart #5

Chart #5 looks more closely at the behavior of housing inflation according to the BLS. In the past month, BLS calculates that OER rose at a 4.7% annualized rate and is up at a 3.9% annualized rate over the previous 3 months. At a time when national home prices are on the verge of falling (and rents are flat to down), the BLS figures that housing inflation is accelerating! Trump is right to want a change in BLS leadership.

Chart #6

Chart #6 shows the 6-mo. annualized change in the CPI versus the ex-shelter version of the CPI. Without OER inflation, the CPI has been increasing at about a 2% annual rate for the past 3 years, and the rate of change has actually fallen to a mere 1.7% over the past six months. This means that the uptick in CPI inflation in August was driven by a big increase in the OER component of the CPI, as shown in Chart #5.

Chart #7

Chart #7 shows the ratio of M2 to nominal GDP. I think that's a good proxy for money demand. Thanks to a big slowdown in M2 growth over the past three years, the amount of readily spendable money in the economy (M2) is almost back to pre-Covid levels. Excess money has been absorbed, and money demand is stabilizing. Thus, there is little or no reason to worry that inflation is coming back to haunt us.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: S&P 500 Snapshot: 4-Day Win Streak Snapped
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The S&P 500 posted four straight days of gains before inching down on Friday. The index ultimately finished the week up 1.6%, marking its fifth weekly gain in the past six weeks. Here is a snapshot of the index from the past week:

The table below summarizes the number of record highs reached each year dating back to 2013.

Here is a snapshot of the index from the past six months with a 50-day moving average:

S&P 500: A Perspective on Drawdowns

On October 9, 2007, the S&P 500 reached an all-time high, closing the day at 1565.15. Then on March 9, 2009, the index dropped ~57% off of its high from exactly 17 months before, closing the day at 676.53. This time period became known as the Global Financial Crisis. It took over 5 years before the index reached a new all-time high on March 28, 2013, where it closed out at 1569.19. The chart below is a snapshot of record highs and selloffs since the 2007 peak reached on October 9, 2007.

What happens if we take out the Global Financial Crisis? Here's a snapshot of the same chart above where the start date has been changed to the trough reached on March 9, 2009. Note the recent selloffs in 2022.

Here are a few tables with the number of days of a 1% or greater change in either direction and the number of days of corrections (down 10% or more from the record high).

And here is a linear chart of the index since October 9, 2007:

Here is a linearly scaled version of the same chart with the 50- and 200-day moving averages. The index has been above the 50-day moving average since May 1st and above the 200-day moving average since May 12th. Additionally, the 50-day moving average has been above the 200-day moving average since July 1st.

S&P 500: A Perspective on Volatility

For a sense of the correlation between the closing price and intraday volatility, the chart below overlays the S&P 500 since 2007 with the intraday price range. On April 9th, the index experienced its largest intraday price volatility (10.77%) since December 24th, 2018 (19.10%). Also included is the 20-day moving average to identify trends in volatility. Over the past 20 days, the average percent change from the intraday low to the intraday high is 0.71%.

S&P 500 versus S&P Equal Weight

The S&P 500 is a market cap-weighted index, which includes roughly the 500 largest U.S. stocks spanning 11 sectors. The S&P 500 Equal Weight Index includes the same constituents as the S&P 500, but each company is equally weighted at a fixed weight. So how do these two indexes match up against each other this year?

The S&P 500 is currently up 12.20% year-to-date, while the S&P Equal Weight is up 7.81% year-to-date.

ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include: iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPLG), and Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Wrapping Up The 2024 SPIVA Institutional Scorecard
data.description:
By Michael Brower

For over 20 years, the S&P Indices versus Active (SPIVA®) U.S. Scorecard has assessed how active mutual fund managers perform against their relevant S&P Dow Jones Indices benchmarks across various timeframes and asset classes. The scorecard’s methodology was enhanced in 2015 to include institutional accounts and was further extended in 2023 to include separately managed wrap accounts (SMAs), which have an annual fee structure that bundles (or wraps) all the administrative, commission and management expenses for the account.

Wrap accounts differ from institutionally focused separate accounts in that they allow smaller market participants to access professional portfolio managers, which were once only available to large institutional investors.1 The SPIVA Institutional Scorecard is important because it facilitates comparisons between institutional and SMA/wrap accounts on a gross-of-fees basis, eliminating any possibility that fees are the sole contributor to a given manager’s underperformance.

Exhibit 1 demonstrates that 55% of equity SMAs/wrap accounts underperformed their benchmarks in 2024, down 5% from 2023. This difference was more pronounced in fixed income, where only 32% of SMAs/wrap accounts underperformed in 2024, but over 65% underperformed in the year prior. Nevertheless, and consistent with past SPIVA research, rates of underperformance for equities and fixed income increased over longer measurement periods. 80% of equity accounts and 57% of fixed income accounts failed to outperform their respective benchmarks over a 10-year period.

Active performance varied considerably by market capitalization. 67% of All Large-Cap SMA/wrap managers underperformed the S&P 500® in 2024, generally consistent with their institutional mutual fund peers. Small-cap managers fared better, with only 19% underperforming their respective benchmarks in 2024, outpacing the 30% underperformance rate in 2023. Managers were perhaps aided by style bias opportunities to tilt toward outperforming large-cap stocks. Last year, The 500™ outpaced the S&P MidCap 400® and S&P SmallCap 600® by 11.1% and 16.3%, respectively.

Turning to fixed income SMA/wrap performance, Exhibit 3 shows that U.S. Aggregate and Core accounts delivered majority outperformance, with only 11% and 13% of accounts failing to outperform their benchmarks, respectively, which was an improvement over their institutional peers. However, Municipal accounts may have had a tougher time, with more than half underperforming the S&P National AMT-Free Municipal Bond Index.

Beating the benchmark can be a challenge regardless of investment vehicle, and SMAs/Wrap accounts are no exception. For a more complete analysis of performance across segregated institutional and SMAs/wrap accounts, we invite you to read our full 2024 SPIVA Institutional Scorecard.

1 Comprehensive definitions for SMAs can be found in the eVestment Alliance Glossary of Terms.

For more information on S&P DJI, please visit www.spdji.com. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 09/13/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.
